does: This paper investigates how AI agents with code generation capabilities perform on economic research tasks and whether expertise in specific domains produces persistent advantages over time. The work constructs benchmarks for agentic coding systems on economic problems and measures how performance scales with domain-specific knowledge injection. The authors evaluate various architectural choices for economic reasoning agents and quantify the degree to which pre-trained models with economic fine-tuning maintain performance advantages relative to general-purpose baselines across multiple research scenarios.
connects: This research builds on the growing literature examining agentic AI systems for specialized domains, extending prior work on code-generating language models beyond generic programming tasks. It connects to earlier findings on domain adaptation in large language models and the persistence of expertise effects in neural architectures. The paper also responds to questions raised in studies of economic forecasting systems and AI-augmented research workflows, combining insights from both the agentic AI and specialized domain modeling literatures to systematically measure transfer and retention of economic knowledge.
enables: This work opens directions for building more sophisticated AI research assistants in specialized fields with measurable expertise requirements. It suggests pathways for developing long-horizon agentic systems where domain knowledge genuinely compounds over time rather than degrading. The findings could inform design choices for AI systems deployed in professional economic research environments and point toward methods for certifying or benchmarking expertise preservation in increasingly autonomous AI agents, potentially enabling new models of human-AI collaboration in knowledge work.
